Just now

“The trophy didn’t fit into the suitcase because it was too large.”
You know “it” means the trophy. Now swap one word:
“The trophy didn’t fit into the suitcase because it was too small.”
Suddenly “it” means the suitcase. Same sentence shape, same pronoun but opposite answer. A human resolves this in the time it takes to read it by weighing physical logic against context. For years, machines couldn’t do this reliably because of how they were reading.
1. Why Reading Word-by-Word Breaks Down
Before the Transformer, most language models were built on Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), and later their improved variant, Long Short-Term Memory networks (LSTMs). The design matched the sequential nature of language: the model read one token at a time, updating a hidden state that carried a summary of everything seen so far.
The problem is how that hidden state gets trained. RNNs learn through backpropagation through time, which means gradients have to flow backward through every previous step. Over a long sequence, that repeated multiplication tends to shrink the gradient until…
